output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: critique of the section, flagging an internal inconsistency about which dataset is used, several over-claims relative to the evidence shown, and missing comparisons/details that would strengthen the methods contribution.

Key issues:

1. Dataset inconsistency. The opening sentence says the analysis is on "the DDSM-Plus cohort used throughout this paper," yet the headline AUC and ECE numbers are reported on a "held-out CSAW-CC split." Either DDSM-Plus is the training cohort and CSAW-CC is an external test set (which would actually be a strength, but you need to say so), or one of the two names is wrong. As written, the reader cannot tell whether the kappa=0.42 statistic governs the training labels, the test labels, both, or neither. Resolve this explicitly, and if it is in fact a cross-cohort evaluation, foreshadow that and explain why Section 4 is still needed.

2. "State-of-the-art" is unsupported. The summary paragraph claims SOTA on BI-RADS 3-vs-4a, but the comparators shown are two internal baselines (majority-vote CE and symmetric label smoothing). No external benchmark, no published prior result, and no confidence interval is reported. Either drop the SOTA claim or cite specific prior numbers on the same split with matching evaluation protocol. A single-seed AUC gap of 0.872 vs 0.858 (≈0.014) is small enough that variance across seeds and bootstrap CIs on the test set matter.

3. The novelty framing for RDL is underspecified. You contrast RDL with (a) symmetric label smoothing and (b) "prior disagreement-aware approaches which typically discard or down-weight high-disagreement cases." That second category is vague — name representative works (e.g., approaches that model annotator-specific heads, Dawid–Skene-style latent-truth models, soft-label distillation from rater distributions, learning-from-crowds methods). Right now it reads as a strawman because the most directly comparable line of work — training on soft rater-distribution targets — is exactly what RDL appears to do, so the delta needs sharper articulation. Is the novelty the *learned per-instance weight*? The *auxiliary disagreement head*? Both? Say so.

4. Mechanism description is thin. "Contributes a regularizer to the main classification loss via a learned per-instance weight" leaves several things ambiguous: is the weight a function of the image, of the predicted disagreement probability, or a free parameter per training example? How is it constrained (sigmoid? softmax over a budget?)? Without these specifics, the result is not reproducible and a reviewer cannot tell whether the gain comes from the loss formulation or from added capacity.

5. Evidence-claim slippage on calibration. ECE improves from 0.071 to 0.039 "on the disagreement subset" — good, but compared to which baseline? Presumably majority-vote CE, but the sentence is ambiguous given that two baselines were just introduced. Also report ECE on the full test set; a method tuned to help the disagreement subset could plausibly worsen calibration elsewhere, and you should preempt that question.

6. Missing methodological hygiene. No mention of: number of seeds, how the per-instance weight was initialized/scheduled, whether the disagreement-head supervision used the same three readers as the kappa computation, train/val/test split sizes, or whether hyperparameters were tuned on a separate validation fold versus the reported test split. With only three readers, the "empirical reader distribution" is a 3-sample histogram per case — this is worth acknowledging as a limitation.

7. Structural suggestion. The section currently goes problem → method → result → summary in three paragraphs. The method paragraph is doing too much (architecture, loss, training recipe, results) while the problem paragraph spends most of its space on motivation already covered in Section 1 (presumably). Consider splitting: a short problem statement, a methods paragraph that fully specifies RDL and the per-instance weight, a results paragraph with baselines + CIs + ablation isolating RDL from the multi-task head, and a brief bridge to Section 4.

8. Smaller prose items. "Our concern in this section is therefore to develop…" → "We therefore develop…". "Standard augmentations apply" is too terse for a methods description; either enumerate or cite an appendix. "Substantially improved calibration" — quantify the substantively (relative reduction of ~45% in ECE on the disagreement subset) rather than leaning on the adjective.

Uncertainty: I have not verified the specific published AUCs for BI-RADS 3-vs-4a on CSAW-CC, the exact composition of CSAW-CC vs DDSM-Plus, or whether a "DDSM-Plus" cohort with three readers is a standard resource — these claims should be checked against the paper's references before publication.
